volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Captives (Section 3.1.465 - Section 3.4.585)

sections.label: Section 3.1.465
sections.original: ita malignitate oneravit omnis mortalis mihi ;
sections.translation: — with such enmity has it filled all people towards me . One more starved out I never did see , nor one more filled with hunger [*] , nor one who prospers less in whatever he begins to do . So much do my stomach and my throat take rest on these fasting holidays [*] . Away with the profession of a Parasite to very utter and extreme perdition !

sections.label: Section 3.1.470
sections.original: ita iuventus iam ridiculos inopesque ab se segregat .
sections.translation: so much in these days do the young men drive away from them the needy drolls . They care nothing now-a-days for these Laconian men [*] of the lowest benches — these whipping-posts , who have their clever sayings without provision and without money . They now-a-days seek those who , when they ’ ve eaten at their pleasure , may give them a return at their own houses . They go themselves to market , which formerly was the province of the Parasites .

sections.label: Section 3.1.475
sections.original: ipsi de foro tam aperto capite ad lenones eunt
sections.translation: They go themselves from the Forum to the procurers with face as exposed [*] as the magistrates in court [*] , with face exposed , condemn those who are found guilty ; nor do they now value buffoons at one farthing [*] ; all are so much in love with themselves . For , when , just now , I went away from here , I came to some young men in the Forum : Good morrow , said I ; whither are we going together to breakfast ? On this , they were silent .

sections.label: Section 3.1.480
sections.original: quis ait hoc aut quis profitetur ? inquam . quasi muti silent ,
sections.translation: Who says , ‘ here , at my house ,’ or who makes an offer ? said I . Just like dumb men , they were silent , and didn ’ t smile at me . Where do we dine ? said I . On this they declined . I said one funny saying out of my best bon mots , by which I formerly used to get feasting for a month ; not an individual smiled ; at once I knew that the matter was arranged by concert .

sections.label: Section 3.1.485
sections.original: ne canem quidem irritatam voluit quisquam imitarier ,
sections.translation: Not even one was willing to imitate a dog when provoked ; if they didn ’ t laugh , they might , at least , have grinned with their teeth [*] . From them I went away , after I saw that I was thus made sport of . I went to some others ; then to some others I came ; then to some others — the same the result . All treat the matter in confederacy , just like the oil-merchants in the Velabrum [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.1.490
sections.original: nunc redeo inde , quoniam mé ibi video ludificarier .
sections.translation: Now , I ’ ve returned thence , since I see myself made sport of there . In like manner do other Parasites walk to and fro , to no purpose , in the Forum . Now , after the foreign fashion [*] , I ’ m determined to enforce all my rights . Those who have entered into a confederacy , by which to deprive us of food and life ,— for them I ’ ll name a day . I ’ ll demand , as the damages , that they shall give me ten dinners

sections.label: Section 3.1.495
sections.original: meo arbitratu dent , cum cara annona sit . sic egero .
sections.translation: at my own option , when provisions are dear : thus will I do . Now I ’ ll go hence to the harbour . There , is my only hope of a dinner ; if that shall fail me , I ’ ll return here to the old gentleman , to his unsavoury dinner .

sections.label: Section 3.2.498
sections.original: Quid est suávius , quam bene rém gerere ,
sections.translation: What is there more delightful than to manage one ’ s own interests well for the public good [*] , just as I did yesterday ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.500
sections.original: cum emi hósce homines : ubi quísque vident ,
sections.translation: when I purchased these men . Every person , as they see me , comes to meet me , and congratulates me on this matter . By thus stopping and detaining unlucky me , they ’ ve made me quite tired . With much ado have I survived [*] from being congratulated , to my misfortune .

sections.label: Section 3.2.505
sections.original: tandem ábii ad praetórem ; ibí vix requiévi :
sections.translation: At last , to the Praetor did I get . There , scarcely did I rest myself . I asked for a passport ; it was given me : at once I delivered it to Tyndarus . He started for home . Thence , straightway , after that was done , I passed by my house ; and I went at once to my brother ’ s , where my other captives are . I asked about Philocrates from Elis , whether any one of them all knew the person .

sections.label: Section 3.2.510
sections.original: noverit : tandem hic exclamat , eum sibi esse sodalem ;
sections.translation: This man ( pointing to ARISTOPHONTES ) called out that he had been his intimate friend ; I told him that he was at my house . At once he besought and entreated me that I would permit him to see him . Forthwith I ordered him to be released from chains . Thence have I come . ( To ARISTOPHONTES .) Now , do you follow me ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.514_515
sections.original: ut quód me oravisti ímpetres , eum hóminem uti convénias .—
sections.translation: that you may obtain what you have besought of me , the opportunity of meeting with this person .

sections.label: Section 3.3.516
sections.original: Nunc íllud est , cum mé fuisse quam ésse nimio mávelim :
sections.translation: Now stands the matter so , that I would much rather that I had once existed , than that I still exist ; now do my hopes , my resources , and my succour , desert me and spurn themselves . This is that day , when , for my life , no safety can be hoped ; nor yet is death my end ; nor hope is there , in fact , to dispel this fear for me ;

sections.label: Section 3.3.520
sections.original: nec subdolis mendaciis mihi usquam mantellum est meis ,
sections.translation: nor cloak have I anywhere for my deceitful stratagems ; nor for my devices or my subterfuges is there anywhere a screen presented to me . No deprecating is there for my perfidy ; no means of flight for my offences . No refuge is there anywhere for my trusting ; and no escape for my cunning schemes . What was concealed is now exposed ; my plans are now divulged .

sections.label: Section 3.3.525
sections.original: ómnis res palam est , néque de hac re negotium est ,
sections.translation: The whole matter is now laid open ; nor is there any ado about this matter , but that I must perish outright , and meet with destruction , both on behalf of my master and myself . This Aristophontes has proved my ruin , who has just now come into the house . He knows me . He is the intimate friend and kinsman of Philocrates . Not Salvation herself [*] can save me now , even if she wishes ; nor have I any means of escape ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.530
sections.original: nisi si áliquam corde machinor astutiam .
sections.translation: unless , perchance , I devise some artifice in my mind . ( He meditates .) Plague on it !- how ? What can I contrive ?— what can I think of ? Some very great folly and trifling I shall have to begin with . I ’ m quite at a loss .

sections.label: Section 3.4.533
sections.original: Quo illúm nunc hominem próripuisse fóras se dicam ex aédibus ?
sections.translation: Whither am I to say , now , that this man has betaken himself from the house out of doors ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.534
sections.original: Núnc enim vero ego óccidi : eúnt ad te hostes , Tyndare .
sections.translation: Now , for a very certainty , I ’ m done for ; the enemies are coming to you , Tyndarus !

sections.label: Section 3.4.535
sections.original: quíd loquar ? quid fabulabor ? quid negabo aut quid fatebor ?
sections.translation: What shall I say ?— what shall I talk of ? What shall I deny , or what confess ? All matters are reduced to uncertainty . How shall I place confidence in my resources ? I wish the Gods had destroyed you , before you were lost to your own country , Aristophontes , who , from a plot well concerted , are making it disconcerted . This plan is ruined outright , unless I find out for myself some extremely bold device .

sections.label: Section 3.4.540
sections.original: Sequere . ém tibi hominem . adi , atque adloquere .
sections.translation: Follow me . See , there is the man ; go to him and address him .

sections.label: Section 3.4.540b
sections.original: Quis homo est me hominum miserior ?
sections.translation: What mortal among mortals is there more wretched than myself ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.541
sections.original: Quíd istuc est quod meos te dicam fugitare oculos , Tyndare ,
sections.translation: Why ’ s this , that I ’ m to say that you are avoiding my gaze , Tyndarus ? And why that you are slighting me as a stranger , as though you had never known me ? Why , I ’ m as much a slave as yourself ; although at home I was a free man , you , even from your childhood , have always served in slavery in Elis .

sections.label: Section 3.4.545
sections.original: Edepol minime miror , si te fugitat aut oculos tuos ,
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I ’ m very little surprised , if either he does avoid your gaze , or if he does shun you , who are calling him Tyndarus , instead of Philocrates .

sections.label: Section 3.4.547
sections.original: Hegió , hic homó rabiosus habitus est in Alide ,
sections.translation: Hegio , this person was accounted a madman in Elis . Don ’ t you give ear to what he prates about ; for at home he has pursued his father and mother with spears ,

sections.label: Section 3.4.550
sections.original: et illic isti qui insputatur morbus interdum venit .
sections.translation: and that malady sometimes comes upon him which is spit out [*] Do you this instant stand away at a distance from him .

sections.label: Section 3.4.551b
sections.original: Vltro istum a me .
sections.translation: Away with him further off from me .

sections.label: Section 3.4.551c
sections.original: Ain , verbero ?
sections.translation: Do you say , you whipp ’ d knave , that I am mad , and do you declare that I have followed my own father with spears ? And that I have that malady , that it ’ s necessary for me to be spit upon [*] ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.554
sections.original: Ne verere , multos iste morbus homines macerat ,
sections.translation: Don ’ t be dismayed ; that malady afflicts many a person

sections.label: Section 3.4.555
sections.original: quibus insputari saluti fuit atque is profuit .
sections.translation: to whom it has proved wholesome to be spit upon , and has been of service to them .

sections.label: Section 3.4.556
sections.original: Quid tu autem ? etiam huic credis ?
sections.translation: Why , what do you say ? Do you , too , credit him ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.556b
sections.original: Quid ego credam huic ?
sections.translation: Credit him in what ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.556c
sections.original: Insanum esse me ?
sections.translation: That I am mad ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.557
sections.original: Viden tu hunc , quam ínimico voltu intuetur ? concedi optumumst ,
sections.translation: Do you see him , with what a furious aspect he ’ s looking at you ? ’ Twere best to retire , Hegio ; it is as I said , his frenzy grows apace ; have a care for yourself .

sections.label: Section 3.4.559
sections.original: Credidi esse insanum extemplo , ubi te appellavit Tyndarum .
sections.translation: I thought that he was mad , the moment that he called you Tyndarus .

sections.label: Section 3.4.560
sections.original: Quin suom ipse interdum ignorat nomen neque scit qui siet .
sections.translation: Why , he ’ s sometimes ignorant of his own name , and doesn ’ t know what it is .

sections.label: Section 3.4.561
sections.original: At etiam te suom sodalem esse aibat .
sections.translation: But he even said that you were his intimate friend .

sections.label: Section 3.4.561b
sections.original: Haud vidi magís .
sections.translation: So far from that , I never saw him . Why , really , Alcmaeon , and Orestes , and Lycurgus [*] besides , are my friends on the same principle that he is .

sections.label: Section 3.4.563b
sections.original: At etiam , furcifer ,
sections.translation: Villain , and do you dare speak ill of me , as well ? Do I not know you ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.564b
sections.original: Pol planum id quidem est ,
sections.translation: I ’ faith , it really is very clear

sections.label: Section 3.4.565
sections.original: non novisse , qui istum appelles Tyndarum pro Philocrate .
sections.translation: that you don ’ t know him , who are calling him Tyndarus , instead of Philocrates . Him whom you see , you don ’ t know ; you are addressing him as the person whom you don ’ t see .

sections.label: Section 3.4.567
sections.original: Immo iste eum sese ait , qui non est , esse , et qui vero est , negat .
sections.translation: On the contrary this fellow ’ s saying that he is the person who he is not ; and he says that he is not the person who he really is .

sections.label: Section 3.4.568
sections.original: Tu énim repertu'srepertus es , Philocratem qui superes veriverbio .
sections.translation: You ’ ve been found , of course , to excel Philocrates in truthfulness .

sections.label: Section 3.4.569
sections.original: Pol ego ut rem video , tu inventu'sinventus es , vera vanitudine
sections.translation: By my troth , as I understand the matter , you ’ ve been found to brazen out the truth by lying . But i ’ faith , prithee , come then , look at me .

sections.label: Section 3.4.570b
sections.original: Em .
sections.translation: Well !

sections.label: Section 3.4.570c
sections.original: Dic modo :
sections.translation: Say , now ; do you deny that you are Tyndarus ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.571b
sections.original: Négo , inquam .
sections.translation: I do deny it , I say .

sections.label: Section 3.4.571c
sections.original: Tun te Philocratem
sections.translation: Do you say that you are Philocrates ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.572b
sections.original: Égo , inquam .
sections.translation: I do say so , I say .

sections.label: Section 3.4.572c
sections.original: Tune huic credis ?
sections.translation: And do you believe him ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.572d
sections.original: Plus quidem quam tibi aut mihi .
sections.translation: More , indeed , than either you or myself . For he , in fact , who you say that he is ( pointing to TYNDARUS ) , has set out hence to-day for Elis , to this person ’ s father .

sections.label: Section 3.4.574b
sections.original: Quem patrem , qui servos est ?
sections.translation: What father , when he ’ s a slave [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.4.574c
sections.original: Et tu quidem
sections.translation: And so are

sections.label: Section 3.4.575
sections.original: servos es , liber fuisti , et ego me confido fore ,
sections.translation: you a slave , and yet you were a free man ; and I trust that so I shall be , if I restore his son here to liberty .

sections.label: Section 3.4.577
sections.original: Quid ais , furcifer ? tun te gnatum esse memoras liberum ?
sections.translation: How say you , villain ? Do you say that you were born a free man [ liber ]?

sections.label: Section 3.4.578
sections.original: Non equidem me Liberum , sed Philocratem esse aio .
sections.translation: I really do not say that I am Liber [*] , but that I am Philocrates .

sections.label: Section 3.4.578b
sections.original: Quid est ?
sections.translation: How ’ s this ? How this scoundrel , Hegio , is making sport of you now .

sections.label: Section 3.4.580
sections.original: nám is est servos ipse , neque praeter se umquam ei servos fuit .
sections.translation: For he ’ s a slave himself , and never , except his own self , had he a slave .

sections.label: Section 3.4.581
sections.original: Quia tute ipse eges in patria nec tibi qui vivas domist ,
sections.translation: Because you yourself are destitute in your own country , and haven ’ t whereon to live at home , you wish all to be found like to yourself ; you don ’ t do anything surprising . ’ Tis the nature of the distressed to be ill-disposed , and to envy the fortunate .

sections.label: Section 3.4.584
sections.original: Hegio , vide sis , ne quid tu huic temere insistas credere .
sections.translation: Hegio , take you care , please , that you don ’ t persist in rashly placing confidence in this man ;

sections.label: Section 3.4.585
sections.original: atque , ut perspicio , profecto iam áliquid pugnae edidit .
sections.translation: for so far as I see , he is certainly now putting some device in execution , in saying that he is redeeming your son from captivity ; that is by no means satisfactory to me .
